🚨 Read this carefully $TAO holders. What happened on @TargonCompute SN4 will change how people think about compute, AI agents, and Bittensor forever.

It's a hardened Linux operating system:

• Download it. 
• Flash it to a USB. 
• Boot your machine. 
• Enter your hotkey. 
• Wait 10 minutes. 
• Done. 

Your RTX 4090, your rack of 3090s, your gaming rig it's now a Targon node earning $TAO.

TargonOS changes everything.

Two massivs announcements. 

TargonOS: Any GPU on earth can now join the network.

Until now, Targon's secure compute required Intel TDX datacenter-grade hardware like H100s, H200s, B200s. That's enterprise security. White paper co-written with Intel. Nobody can see your data, your model weights, or your code. Not even the machine operator.

Limiting the network to a narrow slice of the world's GPUs.

The operator never touches it again:

• No SSH.
• No packages. 
• No Kubernetes. 
• The OS updates itself. 

If an update fails, it rolls back automatically. 

The operator's only job: keep the power on and the internet connected.

Security without TDX works through TPM 2.0 chips standard in virtually every machine built in the last decade. The boot chain is cryptographically verified. Disk encryption keys are split using Shamir's secret sharing across multiple independent servers. If the operator tampers with anything, attestation fails and the machine can't boot.

Two tiers. One platform. Enterprise nodes with Intel TDX for the most sensitive workloads. Community nodes with TPM attestation for everything else. Same API. Same billing. Customer picks hardware class, trust tier follows automatically.

🤔 Think about what this means:

Millions of idle GPUs worldwide that previously had no way to participate can now join Targon with zero technical knowledge. More supply means lower prices for customers. Lower prices means more demand. More demand means more revenue for operators. That's the flywheel.

@the_mattyk from @manifoldlabs said it plainly: "TVM was the first big unlock. TargonOS is the second. The downstream effects will be massive."

Michael Taolor: An AI agent running its entire business on Bittensor subnets.

This is the part that should make your head spin.

@michaeltaolor is an autonomous AI agent 3,044 followers, automated by @taolorlabs that runs agent swarms across Bittensor. Research agents. Newsroom agents. Infrastructure monitors. Each with its own knowledge domain, its own isolated database, its own evolving context.

Here's what the agent's infrastructure actually looks like:

@Targon SN4 for compute. @chutes_ai SN64 for LLM inference. @MacrocosmosAI @Data_SN13 SN13 for social data. @desearch_ai SN22 for web search. Soon @minotaursubnet SN112 for on-chain processes.

Five Bittensor subnets. 
One agent. 

Each subnet is a service the agent calls through a unified API. The more subnets that integrate, the more capable the agent becomes.

The architecture is built around sleep/wake. Agents sleep by default. Wake on demand. At any moment maybe 5-10 are active. The rest cost nothing but storage. Targon's usage-based billing means they only pay for compute when agents are actually working. Estimated 30% cost savings over always-on cloud setups.

And it gets wilder 👀👀👀

The roadmap includes agents spawning sub-agents that spawn their own sub-graphs. 

Autonomous SN mining agent accumulates enough domain knowledge, mines subnets pay for own compute through emissions

Traditional cloud is a landlord: rent from AWS/Azure, pay whether you use it or not, zero leverage.

An open marketplace: any GPU competes on price/performance with cryptographic security. AI agents become economic participants improve themselves.

Targon alone: $105K revenue in 7 days ($5.5M annualized), 176 H200s live, Intel co-authoring research, $10.5M Series A from OSS Capital, Ram Shriram, Tobi Lutke, and DCG.

The $680B cloud market doesn’t shift gradually. Agents do workloads across SN and pay for themselves.

AI Systems aren’t just models… they’re full ecosystems.

Most people think AI = LLM.
But in reality, it’s a complete pipeline working together 👇

1. User → API
Every request starts here.
The API acts as the gateway between user and intelligence.

2. API → LLM
The brain of the system.
Processes prompts, generates outputs, makes decisions.

3. Vector Database (Memory Layer)
Stores embeddings for fast retrieval.
This is what powers context + RAG-based systems.

4. Analysis & Refinement
Improves responses over time.
Includes fine-tuning, prompt optimization, and evaluation.

5. Feedback Loop
User feedback = gold.
Helps correct errors and improve future outputs.

6. Monitoring (Most ignored layer)
Tracks performance, latency, cost, and failures.
Without this, your AI system will silently break.

7. Continuous Learning Cycle
Data → Insights → Updates → Better responses

Real Insight:
If you only focus on the model…
you’ll never build production-ready AI systems.

The real power = how these components work together.

THE ARCHITECTURAL PIVOT: FROM LLM AGGREGATOR TO VERTICAL UTILITY

The "DeAI Summer" of 2026 is currently obsessed with numbers, 200+ models, 34k agents, etc. But if you're only looking at the quantity, you're missing the Structural Transition.

DGrid_AI is no longer just a "Unified Gateway." It is evolving into the first Decentralized Operating System for Vertical AI.

What's not being talked about:
While the feed highlights the AscendEX and CyberCharge partnerships, the real alpha is the "Domain-Specific Fine-Tuning" layer. We’re moving past "General Chatbots" into Verifiable Specialist Agents.

The 3 Pillars of the DGrid_AI Utility Shift:
 ✅ Predictive DePIN (CyberCharge): This isn't just "charging." It's AI agents managing energy load balancing and predictive maintenance. Real-world physical infrastructure running on DGrid_AI logic.
 ✅ Institutional Grade API (AscendEX): Enterprises don't want "cool bots." They want Verifiable Reliability. The new combined API layer means trading desks can now run institutional-grade strategies with the transparency of a blockchain.
 ✅ The Verticalization of ERC-8004: We’re starting to see the birth of "Subject Matter Expert" NFAs. Imagine a Medical Audit Agent or a Legal Compliance Agent whose entire "specialized knowledge" is chiseled into their on-chain reputation.

The Takeaway:
2024 was about the Infrastructure.
2025 was about the Identity.
2026 is about the Execution.

We are shifting from "AI can do anything" to "This specific DGrid_AI Agent is the global best at this specific task." The age of the Generalist is closing. The age of the Verifiable Specialist is live.

_____

The Cannes Pivot: Why ‘Verifiable’ is the Only Word That Matters Tonight

If you’re tracking the side events and mainstage talks in Cannes tonight, you’ll notice a distinct shift in the air. 

We’ve spent years talking about DeFi yields and L2 scaling, but Day 2 of EthCC has made one thing clear: the industry has finally realized that AI is the only endgame that matters, and 0G Labs is the one providing the receipts.

The "Aura" in the Palais des Festivals tonight isn't coming from hype; it’s coming from the demonstration of Sealed Inference. While other projects are still presenting whitepapers on how they might verify AI, the 0G Labs team is showing how they are doing it. The conversation has moved past "Can we put AI on-chain?" to "How do we ensure the AI isn't lying to us?"

By solving the Verification Gap at the hardware level, 0G Labs is effectively bridging the distance between the French Riviera and the future of the global agentic economy. 

It’s no longer about whether decentralized AI can compete with the giants; it’s about the fact that decentralized AI powered by the 0G Labs stack, is the only version that can actually be trusted.

Cannes 2026 will be remembered as the moment AI stopped being a "side quest" for Web3 and became the main mission. The infrastructure is here. The math is verified. The Bureau is open.

DGrid.AI is a decentralized AI inference network designed to create an open, accessible, low cost, and community powered infrastructure for running large language models (LLMs), AI agents, and related services. Built with blockchain principles at its core, it addresses key limitations of centralized AI providers (such as OpenAI and similar giants) by offering trustless, verifiable, and distributed compute resources.

The Problem DGrid.AI Aims to Solve

The rapid growth of AI has created an explosion of models proprietary, open source, and fine tuned but the ecosystem suffers from significant inefficiencies:

Fragmentation: Inconsistent interfaces, varying quality, and difficult integration make it costly and time consuming for developers to discover, test, and use different models

Key problems in the current AI landscape that DGrid_AI targets include:

1. Fragmentation: An explosion of proprietary, open source, and fine tuned models/agents with inconsistent interfaces, making integration costly and complex for developers.

2.Centralization and Black Box Issues: Reliance on a handful of providers leads to high costs, single points of failure, censorship risks, unverifiable outputs, and imbalanced value distribution (creators and compute providers capture limited profits).

3. Lack of Trust and Verifiability: Inference results are often opaque, with no easy way to audit quality or ensure consistency.

Economic Inefficiencies: Middleman fees, high integration barriers, and difficulty matching optimal models to tasks.

By leveraging blockchain, DGrid.AI introduces decentralized routing, on chain verification, and incentive alignment to create a resilient, merit based system.

Architecture and How It Works

DGrid.AI operates as a three tier system:

Decentralized Routing & Verification Network  The core "smart gateway."

LLM & Agent Free Market An open marketplace for models and agents.

AI DAO Governance System  Community driven decision making

            Key Components

1. Smart Gateway / Unified API: Developers integrate once via a single API (DGridRPC, a standardized JSON RPC protocol) to access hundreds of models and agents. Intelligent routing automatically directs requests to the best provider based on task type, cost, latency, historical performance, and other factors. This eliminates the need for multiple integrations. 

2. DGrid Nodes: Community operated nodes (run by individuals or operators contributing GPU/compute resources) execute inference tasks. Nodes host various models depending on hardware capacity, report metrics (e.g., latency, compute usage), and earn rewards. The network uses elastic scheduling for scalability and resilience.

3. Proof of Quality (PoQ): A verification mechanism that evaluates outputs on accuracy, consistency, format compliance, etc. Verification nodes randomly sample tasks, and results (with quality scores) can be recorded on chain for auditability and traceability. Poor performance leads to penalties (e.g., slashing of staked tokens).

On Chain Mechanisms: Billing contracts handle automated settlements in $DGAI. A decentralized data availability layer supports auditing. Security features include immutable runtimes, resource controls, and cryptographic request verification (e.g., EIP 712 signatures with pre payment).

Free Market: Model creators list their LLMs/agents, set prices, and receive direct compensation. High performing models can potentially be tokenized for ongoing value capture.

The 2x ATR Rule: How AI Agents Just Solved The Biggest Problem In Algorithmic Trading

algorithmic trading just entered a new dimension where the speed of your code matters less than the intelligence of your logic. if jim simons had access to claude code back in the day he would have reached thirty billion much faster than he did. this is the single biggest opportunity for the individual trader right now but only if you understand how to harness the machine to protect your capital from the chaos of the markets

i believe that code is the great equalizer because i have been on the other side of the screen losing money to liquidations and over trading. back in the day i spent hundreds of thousands of dollars on developers for apps because i was convinced i could not code myself. then i realized that letting others build your trading brain is a recipe for disaster and i had to learn to build my own systems if i ever wanted to stop getting liquidated

the first major hurdle every algorithmic trader faces is volatility and how it destroys even the most elegant strategies. most bots work perfectly when the market is sideways or trending gently but they get chopped to pieces when a news event hits or a sudden spike occurs. the logic i have been developing lately solves this by using the average true range to essentially tell the bot when it is time to sit on its hands and wait for the storm to pass

measuring the average true range allows us to see exactly how much the price is moving compared to its historical norm over a specific period. i have my bots looking at a two hour window of data to calculate what a normal one minute bar looks like. if the recent range in the last five minutes is moving twice as fast as that average the bot knows the market is in a state of high volatility

this protection mechanism is crucial because in times of high volatility many strategies lose their edge and start taking trades they should not. by implementing a volatility shutdown the bot cancels all open orders and closes all positions before going into a forced sleep mode. i have set this up so it shows a countdown every few minutes so i know the bot is still alive but just being patient until the environment is safe again

with bots you must iterate to success which is why i decided to learn everything live and share the process as it happens. i initially set my volatility multiplier to two but realized that might be too aggressive for certain markets. after testing the numbers i found that a one point five multiplier offers a more conservative shield for the capital especially when the bot is looking at two hours of data to establish its baseline

the beauty of this system is that it handles the emergency exits for you so you do not have to stare at the charts in a panic. many traders think they can just manually intervene when things get wild but human emotion is the fastest way to blow an account. code does not get scared and it does not hesitate to close a position if the math says the volatility is exceeding our safety threshold

while pausing during volatility is a great way to save money the real alpha comes from figuring out how to trade during those spikes. i have been building a new set of bots that do not just shut down when the average true range spikes. instead they identify if the market is trending in a specific direction during that high volatility and they look for bullish or bearish pressure to catch a ride on the trend

if the market is moving outside its normal range but showing a clear upward trend the bot will put in test orders to see if the buy side is filling faster than the sell side. if the ask fills it confirms there is bullish pressure and the bot takes a long position to capture the momentum. this turns a period of danger into a period of high reward by using the volatility as a signal rather than just a warning

the infrastructure behind these bots is actually quite simple once you break it down into the core functions. i focus on the plumbing like getting the websocket data and monitoring the position status rather than just looking at fancy indicators. understanding how the bot speaks to the exchange through the api is the only way to ensure that your orders actually hit the book when they are supposed to

it is easy to get distracted by the newest shiny object in the crypto world like launching tokens on pump fun or chasing the next meme. however the regulatory risk for us citizens is often overlooked and can lead to a massive headache that is not worth the potential gain. i decided to avoid the temptation of launching tokens because the risk of being labeled a promoter is far too high when you are trying to build a clean long term business

staying white hat and focusing on building tools that actually provide value is a much better path than chasing short term gambles. i believe that the only sustainable way to make it in this game is to treat it like a technical craft rather than a casino. if you focus on the logic and the code the money will eventually follow as a byproduct of your systems being better than the average participant

learning to code for yourself is the ultimate move because it means you never have to hire anyone again. you do not have to wait for a developer to fix a bug or implement a new idea because you have the power to do it in minutes. this independence is what allowed me to move from being an okay coder to someone who can build anything with the help of ai

the goal of algorithmic trading is to remove all in game decisions so you can live your life while the machines do the work. if you are still staring at the charts for eight hours a day you have not truly automated your life yet. true automation means trusting your systems enough to walk away knowing that the risk management is hardcoded into every single line

as we continue to develop these agents the gap between what a retail trader can do and what an institution can do is shrinking. the same tools that were once reserved for the giants on wall street are now available to anyone with a laptop and the discipline to learn. i will keep sharing the progress and the code because i truly believe that we are just at the beginning of this revolution

the key is to never stop learning and never stop testing new ideas against the harsh reality of the market. every liquidation i suffered was just an expensive lesson that led to a better line of code. if you stay persistent and keep refining your systems you will eventually build a machine that provides the freedom you have been looking for since you started trading

Every country is racing to build AI. India should be racing to deploy it.

And nobody in power understands the difference.

India's AI strategy reads like it was written by academics who have never stepped inside a factory in Ludhiana.

Rs 10,000 crore for AI research. Centres of Excellence at IITs. National AI Mission. Innovation hubs. Policy papers. Committees. More committees reviewing the committees.

Meanwhile, a garment factory owner in Tirupur still confirms orders by calling his buyer in Germany and reading out line items from a notebook. A CA firm in Nagpur still sends article clerks to physically collect documents from clients in 2026. A lawyer in Tis Hazari still maintains case diaries in a register that looks exactly like the one his father used in 1987.

India does not have an AI research problem. India has an AI deployment problem.

And the gap between the two is where this country's future will be decided.

Let me be specific about what I mean.

The US builds foundation models. OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Meta. Billions of dollars in compute. The frontier of what AI can do. Fair enough. That is their game. They have the capital, the talent concentration, and the infrastructure. India will not out-build the US on foundation models. Nor does it need to.

China doesn't build the best models either. Yes China replicates the results with cheaper models, but it does something even more dangerous. China deploys.

A factory owner in Shenzhen doesn't know what a transformer architecture is. He doesn't care. But his production line has AI quality inspection that catches defects his workers miss. His supply chain has demand forecasting that adjusts procurement automatically. His buyer communication is handled by an AI agent that responds in English, Arabic, and Spanish. His invoicing is automated end to end.

He didn't hire AI researchers. He hired deployment people. Engineers who walked into his factory, watched his workflow for a week, and connected existing AI tools to his existing processes.

China has over 400,000 AI deployment professionals. Not researchers. Deployers. People who take models that already exist and make them useful inside real businesses.

India? India has committees discussing whether to build a sovereign LLM.

This is the gap. And it is widening every single day.

India has 63 million MSMEs. 31% of GDP. 250 million jobs. The backbone of this country.

AI deployment in these businesses: essentially zero. Not 7%. Not 2%. Zero.

India's large enterprises are moving. 47% of them have AI running in production (EY-CII, 2025). Infosys, TCS, Reliance, the Tatas. They have technology budgets. They have AI teams. They will be fine.

Their MSME suppliers, distributors, and vendors? Still on Excel. Still on phone calls. Still on WhatsApp. Still running businesses worth crores on tools designed for personal messaging.

The government's response to this? Skill India centres teaching Python to college students who will then fight over 120,000 IT fresher jobs that used to be 600,000 three years ago.

Nobody is asking the obvious question: what if instead of teaching 10 million students to code, we taught 1 million domain experts to deploy AI into the businesses they already understand?

The CA who can automate his own firm's workflow. The factory manager who can connect his inventory system to an AI demand forecaster. The export house owner's daughter who can create multilingual buyer presentations with HeyGen and close international orders. The clinic receptionist who can build a patient intake system with Claude Code.

These people don't need 4-year degrees. They need 3 months of focused training on the tools that already exist.

Claude Code. Cursor. HeyGen. ElevenLabs. Kling. OpenClaw. Hermes.

The tools are here. Most of them are free or under Rs 5,000 a month. The foundation models are built. The APIs are open. The automation platforms are ready.

What doesn't exist is the human bridge. The person who walks into a business, understands the pain, and connects the AI to the workflow.

India doesn't need 10,000 AI researchers. India needs 10 lakh AI deployment professionals.

And the government hasn't even named this category yet.

Look at what China did differently.

China's "AI Plus" initiative doesn't fund research labs. It funds deployment into specific industries. Manufacturing. Agriculture. Healthcare. Logistics. The stated goal is not "advance the frontier of AI." The stated goal is "deploy AI into 50% of enterprises by 2027."

They are not trying to win the Nobel Prize. They are trying to win Tuesday afternoon in a factory in Guangdong.

India's AI mission? "Position India as a global leader in AI innovation."

Innovation. Not deployment. Not adoption. Not making sure the kirana store owner in Jaipur can automate his inventory.

Innovation.

This is not a criticism of research. India needs AI researchers. But India needs AI deployers 100x more urgently. And the ratio of investment is completely inverted.

For every rupee spent on building a new model at an IIT lab, Rs 100 should be spent on teaching a CA in Surat to deploy AI in his practice. For every PhD funded in machine learning, 500 domain experts should be trained to connect Claude Code to Tally.

The math is simple.

India's AI research, even if spectacularly successful, will produce models that compete with what OpenAI and Anthropic release for free. The output of a Rs 10,000 crore research programme will be a model that is roughly as good as what any developer can access via API for $20 a month.

India's AI deployment, if done at scale, would transform 63 million businesses, secure 250 million jobs, and add potentially 15-20% to GDP within a decade.

One path gives us academic papers. The other gives us economic transformation.

We are funding the papers.

The saddest part? The people who could lead this deployment revolution are already out there. They are the domain experts in every industry who know exactly where the pain is. They don't need the government to build them an AI model. They need someone to show them that the model already exists and teach them how to connect it to their WhatsApp, their Tally, their email, their production schedule.

Three months of training. That's it.

But there is no national programme for this. No Skill India module for AI deployment. No MSME ministry initiative to put an AI workflow architect into every district's industrial cluster. No scheme that says: here are 500 deployers trained in Tirupur's garment workflow, 500 in Ludhiana's manufacturing workflow, 500 in Surat's diamond and textile workflow.

Instead we get: another AI hackathon at IIT Bombay.

The US builds the engine. China deploys the engine into every factory. India holds conferences about the engine.

This has to change. And it will change. Not because of policy. Because of economics.

The first wave of Indian AI deployers is already emerging. Not from IITs. Not from government programmes. From necessity. The CA who was drowning in March. The exporter who was losing buyers to faster Chinese competitors. The lawyer who couldn't keep up with her case load. The factory owner's son who saw his father work 14-hour days doing things a machine could do in seconds.

They are teaching themselves. Finding the tools. Building the workflows. Solving real problems in real businesses.

They are India's real AI strategy. Even if nobody in Delhi knows they exist.

The race is not about who builds the best AI.

The race is about who deploys it into the most businesses, the fastest.

China understood this 5 years ago. India still hasn't.

And every day we spend debating sovereign LLMs instead of deploying existing ones into 63 million MSMEs is a day we fall further behind.

India's AI future will not be decided in a research lab.

It will be decided on the factory floor in Ludhiana. In the CA's office in Nagpur. In the export house in Surat. In the district court in Saket.

The only question is whether we get there in time.

Phase 1: Foundations (2–4 weeks)

  - K-Means, PCA

  - Bias-Variance Tradeoff
  - Overfitting, Cross-validation

Phase 3: Practical ML (4–6 weeks)

- Scikit-learn (pipelines, preprocessing)
- Feature engineering
- Model evaluation (F1, ROC, confusion matrix)
- Work on datasets (Kaggle / UCI)

Phase 4: Deep Learning (4–8 weeks)

- Neural Networks, Backpropagation
- Frameworks: PyTorch / TensorFlow
- CNNs (images), RNNs/Transformers (text)

  - Image classifier

Pick ONE based on interest:

- AI/LLMs → Transformers, RAG, fine-tuning
- Data Science → EDA, storytelling, dashboards
- MLOps → deployment, Docker, CI/CD

Phase 6: Real-World Edge

- Build end-to-end projects:
  - Data → Model → API → UI
- Deploy (Streamlit / FastAPI)
- Learn basics of system design for ML

Golden Rules:

- Don’t just watch — build
- Don’t just build — deploy
- Don’t just deploy — explain

Existing approaches to LLM routing often rely on annotated datasets, which restricts the router’s adaptability to tasks where ground-truth labels are scarce or unavailable. What if we could generate training data for a new task? RGD explores exactly this scenario ➡️ we show that routers can be trained on data generated by the model pool itself!

Clarifai 12.3: Introducing KV Cache-Aware Routing! 🚀

LLM inference with multiple replicas typically uses random load balancing. But replicas aren't interchangeable. Each builds up different KV cache state from the requests it processes.

When requests land on replicas without relevant context cached, models recompute everything from scratch. This wastes GPU cycles and increases latency.

KV Cache-Aware Routing automatically detects prompt overlap and routes requests to replicas with relevant cache state already loaded. Shared system prompts are computed once and reused. RAG context is cached when multiple users query similar documents.

Zero configuration required. Deploy a model with multiple replicas and it works.

Also new: Warm Node Pools for faster scaling, Session-Aware Routing to keep user requests on the same replica, Prediction Caching for identical inputs, and  Clarifai Skills for AI coding assistants.

I wanted to understand what it's actually doing under the hood — so I built LLM streaming from scratch using raw Web Streams API

here's exactly how it works:

Groq returns a ReadableStream from the response body. you get a reader from it, a TextDecoder to convert binary chunks to strings, and a buffer to accumulate them

on every tick:
— read the next chunk
— decode it with { stream: true } so multi-byte characters don't get split across chunks
— append to buffer
— update only the last message in React state using the functional updater form

the functional updater is the key part — setMessages(p => ...) instead of setMessages(messages) means you're always working off the latest state, not a stale closure from when the stream started

without it, rapid chunk updates will silently overwrite each other and you'll lose tokens

works with any LLM API that returns a ReadableStream — Groq, OpenAI, Anthropic, whatever

no library. no abstraction. just the platform.

A baseline image classifier hitting 49.71% accuracy on CIFAR-10 jumps to 85.7% when an AI research agent runs the full experiment pipeline autonomously. That is not a human researcher iterating. That is Meta's MLGym framework letting an LLM agent design, implement, and optimize ML experiments through reinforcement learning. Source: MLGym benchmark results, arXiv:2502.14499.

Meta FAIR and GenAI published MLGym in February 2025, led by researchers including Roberta Raileanu and Deepak Nathani. The core idea is deceptively simple: turn the entire machine learning research process into a Gymnasium environment where an AI agent gets a scalar reward for improving task metrics. The agent observes the current state, writes code, runs experiments in a Docker sandbox, analyzes results, and iterates. The same loop a human researcher follows, but automated and trainable with any RL algorithm.

The framework includes 13 open ended research tasks spanning computer vision, natural language processing, reinforcement learning, game theory, and algorithmic reasoning. Each task provides starter code, a dataset, and an evaluation script. The agent must improve on the baseline by proposing new approaches, implementing them, and validating results. Built in tools include literature search via the Semantic Scholar API, PDF parsing, and a persistent memory module.

What makes MLGym different from earlier automated research attempts is the RL integration. Previous systems like Sakana AI's AI Scientist generated papers but could not learn from experimental outcomes. MLGym's Gym interface provides a reward signal tied directly to performance metrics, enabling policy optimization over long research trajectories. The agent learns which experimental strategies work not from human feedback but from actual results.

The benchmark results from frontier models running zero shot (no RL training yet) already show substantial gains. Beyond CIFAR-10, the MountainCar reinforcement learning task went from a baseline reward of 33.79 to 96.34 under agent control. Language modeling validation loss improved from 4.673 to 3.966. A 3-SAT solver runtime dropped from 16.158 seconds to 13.652 seconds. These improvements came from agents autonomously selecting hyperparameters, modifying architectures, and implementing novel training strategies.

I see MLGym as infrastructure rather than a finished product. The current evaluations use base LLMs without RL fine tuning. The real breakthrough will come when agents are trained with PPO or similar algorithms over thousands of research episodes, building cumulative knowledge about which experimental strategies generalize. This is the same scaling dynamic that took AlphaZero from random play to superhuman performance: give the agent a clear reward signal and enough iterations.

The practical limitation is that MLGym handles the experimental core of research but not the full pipeline. It does not generate LaTeX manuscripts or conduct peer review style evaluation. The agent produces experimental artifacts, code, and results that a downstream system could turn into papers. For the research community, the bigger question is whether RL trained research agents will eventually surpass human researchers in domains where experimental iteration speed matters more than creative intuition.

Does automating the experimental loop free researchers to focus on higher level scientific questions, or does it begin the process of making human researchers optional in empirical ML?

@Shopify has replaced its merchant data extraction pipeline, previously built on OpenAI's GPT-5, with a multi-agent framework powered by a self-hosted Qwen 3 model developed by Alibaba Cloud, reducing its per-unit LLM costs by a factor of 75 while doubling the quality of automated outputs. The shift was disclosed by applied machine learning engineer Kshetrajna Raghavan at the Bay Area DSPy Meetup.

Raghavan said "Moving from a single-shot GPT-5 prompt to a multi-agent architecture fundamentally changed our inference economics," and that "Replacing the proprietary API with a self-hosted Qwen 3 model reduced our per-unit LLM costs by 75 times." The new system uses DSPy, an open-source framework from Stanford University that algorithmically optimises language model prompts, breaking the extraction process into specialised agent workflows for tasks like fraud detection and tax coding rather than relying on one model to process entire websites at once.

Raghavan added that implementing the framework meant the team "doubled our quality compared to the previous single-prompt baseline," with the specialised agents also reducing hallucination rates and improving task adherence across Shopify's merchant-facing applications.
